trending.title: CADE Investigates Alleged Algorithmic Collusion Between Gol and LATAM on Domestic Air Routes
trending.body: On 29 April 2026, CADE's Superintendency-General formally opened an administrative proceeding (Projeto Cérebro) to investigate alleged price-fixing between Gol and LATAM on domestic Brazilian air routes, with a focus on algorithmic pricing tools and shared market-intelligence infrastructure. CADE indicated that automated pricing systems and high information transparency in concentrated markets amplify competitive risks beyond traditional collusion. The case sits at the intersection of competition law and AI landscape (algorithmic collusion via AI-driven pricing), triggering its classification as trending. For Prosus portfolio companies such as iFood that operate platform-based dynamic pricing in Brazil, this signals that CADE is developing an enforcement posture on AI-assisted price coordination — compliance audits of algorithmic pricing stacks are advisable.

trending.title: TikTok: Irish Supreme Court Stays €530m GDPR Fine Pending Appeal While ByteDance Faces DSA Addictive-Design Charges
trending.body: On 30 April 2026, the Irish Supreme Court upheld TikTok's application to stay enforcement of the DPC's €530 million GDPR fine — issued 2 May 2025 for unlawful EEA-to-China data transfers in breach of Article 46(1) (€485m) and Article 13(1)(f) (€45m) — allowing transfers to continue during the High Court appeal on the merits. Separately, the European Commission's preliminary DSA finding of 6 February 2026 cited TikTok's addictive design features (infinite scroll, autoplay, notifications) as a systemic risk to minors under the Digital Services Act, with a potential fine of up to 6% of global annual turnover. The item crosses privacy (GDPR enforcement) and AI landscape (algorithmic recommender-system regulation), making it trending. ByteDance's mounting regulatory burden across GDPR, DSA, and its 19.9%-capped US restructuring has read-across for Prosus, which must monitor similar multi-jurisdiction stacking risks for its own portfolio platforms.

trending.title: EU DMA First Statutory Review: Commission Confirms Fit-for-Purpose, Flags AI and Cloud as Next Gatekeeper Priority
trending.body: On 28 April 2026, the European Commission published its first statutory review of the Digital Markets Act (COM(2026) 178), concluding the DMA remains fit for purpose and requires no legislative revision. The review confirms seven non-compliance proceedings have been initiated — two concluded with fines on Apple (€500m, April 2025) and Meta (€200m, April 2025) for breach of the anti-steering obligation and the 'consent or pay' model respectively — while four remain ongoing. The review explicitly flags AI and cloud computing as requiring 'rigorous supervision' going forward, and notes two specification proceedings still open including one on Alphabet/Google Search data-sharing under Article 8(2) DMA with a binding decision expected by 27 July 2026. This item crosses competition (gatekeeper enforcement) and AI landscape (AI access parity), making it trending. For Prosus portfolio companies relying on app stores, search distribution, or cloud infrastructure from designated gatekeepers, the continued DMA enforcement posture heightens interoperability and fair-access obligations.

trending.title: Meta Faces Major Publishers' Class Action for Willful Copyright Infringement in Llama AI Training
trending.body: On 5 May 2026, five major publishing houses — Elsevier, Cengage, Hachette Book Group, Macmillan, and McGraw Hill — together with author Scott Turow filed a putative class action in the US District Court for the Southern District of New York against Meta Platforms and CEO Mark Zuckerberg, alleging willful copyright infringement in training Meta's Llama large language models. The complaint alleges Meta knowingly torrented millions of copyrighted works from pirate sites such as LibGen and Anna's Archive, removed copyright management information to conceal infringement, and that Zuckerberg personally authorised the strategy after abandoning licensing negotiations. The plaintiffs seek statutory damages, a permanent injunction, an order to destroy infringing copies, and an itemised list of all works used. This item crosses IP law (copyright infringement at scale) and AI landscape (training-data legality), making it a clear trending entry. For Prosus portfolio companies deploying or integrating LLMs — including Stack Overflow and Brainly — the case reinforces the legal exposure of unlicensed training-data practices across the AI value chain.

competition.title: CMA Commences Strategic Market Status Investigation into Microsoft's Business Software Ecosystem in May 2026
competition.body: On 31 March 2026, the CMA announced it would launch a Strategic Market Status (SMS) investigation into Microsoft's business software ecosystem — including Copilot — commencing in May 2026 under the UK's Digital Markets, Competition and Consumers Act. The investigation can run up to nine months, after which an SMS designation could enable the CMA to impose conduct requirements or pro-competition interventions targeting Microsoft's licensing practices in cloud. The CMA explicitly framed the action around AI-driven reshaping of competition in productivity software, making Microsoft's bundling of Copilot into enterprise suites central to the probe. For Prosus portfolio companies using Microsoft enterprise software and cloud, potential interoperability or licensing-conduct requirements arising from this investigation could affect commercial terms.

competition.title: DOJ Google Search Antitrust Remedies: Technical Committee Operating, Government Appeals for Structural Relief
competition.body: Following the September 2025 federal court remedies order in United States v. Google, which prohibits exclusive search-distribution contracts and mandates data-sharing with rivals, Google's Final Judgment took effect in early December 2025 with a 60-day grace period. By January 2026, the court appointed the Technical Committee to oversee compliance, and on 3 February 2026 the DOJ and majority of states filed cross-appeals challenging the court's refusal to order structural remedies including sale of Chrome and bans on default placement. As of May 2026 the appellate track is active, creating uncertainty over whether more far-reaching remedies — potentially disrupting Google's default agreements with OEMs and browsers — will be imposed. Prosus and its portfolio companies (including iFood and Swiggy) that rely on Google search and Play distribution channels face continued exposure to changing terms as compliance obligations bite.

competition.title: EU Commission Opens DMA Specification Proceedings on Alphabet/Google Search Data-Sharing with Binding Decision Due July 2026
competition.body: On 27 January 2026, the European Commission opened specification proceedings against Alphabet under Article 8(2) of the DMA (Regulation (EU) 2022/1925), targeting Google Search's data-sharing obligations with rivals. A public consultation on preliminary measures closed on 1 May 2026, and the Commission is expected to issue a final binding decision by 27 July 2026. If adopted, the decision would require Google to open search index and user-interaction data to competitors — a structural access remedy that could reshape the EU search market. For Prosus portfolio companies operating in the EU's digital ecosystem, a binding data-sharing specification would represent one of the most operationally significant DMA enforcement steps to date.

competition.title: Swiggy Wins Karnataka HC Extension of CCI Antitrust Stay Over Confidential Data-Sharing Order
competition.body: On 21 April 2026, the Karnataka High Court extended its interim stay on the Competition Commission of India (CCI) antitrust proceedings against Swiggy, which stem from the National Restaurant Association of India (NRAI) complaint alleging anti-competitive behaviour by Swiggy and Zomato in food delivery. The court raised concerns about evidentiary standards — specifically, the reliability of third-party statements not tested through cross-examination — and continued the stay to protect Swiggy from being compelled to share confidential app data with NRAI representatives pending the merits hearing. The stay has been in place since 17 March 2026 and represents a significant procedural win for Swiggy in a probe that could, if upheld, result in CCI intervention in platform pricing and data-access practices. The outcome matters directly for Prosus, which holds a significant stake in Swiggy.

competition.title: OLX Poland Faces Warsaw Court Upholding UOKiK Fine Over Confusing Rating System
competition.body: On 19 April 2026, a Warsaw court upheld UOKiK's fine of over PLN 28 million against OLX Group for operating a confusing consumer rating system on OLX.pl — the challenged practice ran from November 2020 to July 2025. The UOKiK decision found that OLX's rating mechanism misled consumers about the reliability and origin of ratings, violating Polish consumer protection law. The ruling follows a 2024 UOKiK decision requiring OLX to change its 'Protection Package' practices and issue refunds, indicating repeated regulatory friction for the platform in Poland. OLX is a core Prosus portfolio asset and continued enforcement actions in Poland expose the group to reputational and financial risk in one of its important CEE markets.

competition.title: FTC to Host 'Litigate the Fix' Workshop on Merger Remedy Proposals — 20 May 2026
competition.body: The US Federal Trade Commission announced a public workshop titled 'Eleventh-Hour Antitrust Remedy Proposals and Litigating the Fix' scheduled for 20 May 2026. The event signals the FTC's intent to scrutinise last-minute behavioural remedy offers from merging parties and to reinforce a preference for structural remedies or litigation rather than accepting late-stage fix-it-first proposals. This posture has direct read-across for any Prosus or Naspers portfolio M&A transactions requiring HSR filings in the US, as the FTC is signalling reduced tolerance for complex behavioural commitments.

privacy.title: CNIL Hosts G7 Data Protection Roundtable in Paris — Enforcement Cooperation on Agenda for June 2026
privacy.body: On 6 May 2026, the CNIL announced it will host the G7 Data Protection and Privacy Authorities Roundtable in Paris from 23–26 June 2026, chaired by the CNIL President. 'Enforcement Cooperation' is explicitly listed as a working-group theme, signalling that G7 DPAs are coordinating cross-border enforcement strategies ahead of a busy second half of 2026. The event reflects the broader trajectory of international DPA collaboration under GDPR, LGPD, and POPIA frameworks, which is increasingly relevant for Prosus portfolio companies (PayU, iFood, Swiggy) that process personal data across G7-adjacent jurisdictions. Companies should expect coordinated enforcement actions and information-sharing among G7 DPAs to intensify following the June roundtable.

privacy.title: India DPDP Rules 2025: MeitY Proposes Compressing Compliance Timeline to 12 Months for Significant Data Fiduciaries
privacy.body: The Digital Personal Data Protection Rules, 2025 (notified 13 November 2025) established an 18-month phased compliance window for most processing obligations, placing full enforcement activation at 13 May 2027. However, India's Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY) has proposed compressing the timeline for Significant Data Fiduciaries (SDFs) to 12 months — bringing key obligations forward to approximately November 2026. This acceleration would require SDFs to have consent management, data-localisation controls, and breach-notification processes operational by late 2026, well ahead of the baseline. Prosus portfolio companies operating in India at scale — Swiggy, Meesho, PharmEasy, and Rapido — are strong candidates for SDF designation and should treat November 2026 as the de-facto planning deadline.

privacy.title: EDPB Launches 2026 Coordinated Enforcement Action on GDPR Transparency and Information Obligations
privacy.body: On 19 March 2026, the EDPB officially launched its fifth Coordinated Enforcement Framework (CEF 2026), engaging 25 Data Protection Authorities across Europe in a coordinated assessment of controllers' compliance with GDPR Articles 12, 13, and 14 — the 'right to be informed' transparency cluster. Participating DPAs will conduct both enforcement actions and fact-finding exercises, with national findings consolidated into an EDPB report in the second half of 2026 that will drive targeted follow-up at both national and EU level. The initiative represents the broadest simultaneous GDPR supervisory sweep since the EDPB's 2022 cookie-consent action. Prosus portfolio companies with EU user bases — OLX, eMAG, GoStudent — must review their privacy notices and consent flows against Articles 12–14 immediately.

privacy.title: ANPD Publishes 2026-2027 Priority Enforcement Map: Children's Data, AI, and Public-Sector Processing in Focus
privacy.body: On 24 December 2025, Brazil's ANPD published Resolution CD/ANPD No. 30, approving its 'Mapa de Temas Prioritários' for the 2026–2027 biennium. The four priority themes are: (1) data subject rights; (2) protection of children and adolescents online; (3) public-sector personal data processing; and (4) artificial intelligence and emerging technologies. ANPD committed to conducting at least 25 supervisory activities under theme one and ten activities focused on biometric, health, and financial data. The AI and children's-data themes are of particular relevance to Prosus-affiliated entities: iFood's delivery-data practices and GoStudent's student data processing both fall squarely within ANPD's stated 2026–2027 supervision perimeter.

privacy.title: South Africa Information Regulator Issues Infringement Notices with Fines Up to R5 Million Under POPIA
privacy.body: In November 2025, South Africa's Information Regulator issued three Infringement Notices against bodies that failed to comply with prior Enforcement Notices under Section 95 of POPIA: Blouberg Municipality (R500,000 fine for exposing a former employee's personal data online), Lancet Laboratories (R100,000 fine), and the Department of Basic Education (R5 million fine). The Regulator simultaneously launched a nationwide POPIA compliance monitoring exercise requiring selected organisations to submit detailed compliance reports within 14 business days, covering lawful processing, direct marketing, and cross-border transfers. For Prosus-affiliated entities with South African operations — Takealot and Property24 — the Regulator's escalating enforcement posture under POPIA Section 95 demands immediate review of data governance frameworks.

ip.title: Publishers Class Action Against Meta for Llama Training Data Copyright Infringement Filed in SDNY
ip.body: On 5 May 2026, five major publishers (Elsevier, Cengage, Hachette, Macmillan, McGraw Hill) and author Scott Turow filed a class-action complaint in the US District Court for the Southern District of New York, alleging Meta willfully infringed millions of copyrighted works — including books, textbooks, and journal articles — by torrenting pirate collections (LibGen, Anna's Archive, Sci-Hub) to train its Llama AI models, with Zuckerberg personally authorising the decision after abandoning licensing negotiations. The complaint seeks statutory damages, a permanent injunction, destruction of infringing copies, and disclosure of all copyrighted works used in Llama training. Unlike prior author suits focused on model outputs, this action focuses specifically on the training-data copying as the act of infringement, significantly broadening the legal theory. For Prosus entities deploying or building on LLMs — Stack Overflow (AI Answers), Brainly — the case underscores the imperative to audit third-party AI supplier training-data provenance.

ip.title: European Parliament Adopts Resolution Calling for Itemised AI Training-Data Transparency and Crawl Records
ip.body: On 10 March 2026, the European Parliament adopted an own-initiative resolution — 'Copyright and Generative Artificial Intelligence: Opportunities and Challenges' — demanding that AI providers and deployers publish an itemised list of all copyrighted works used to train their models and maintain detailed records of crawling activities used for inference and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG). The resolution invokes rights-holders' existing opt-out rights and calls for a collective licensing framework to be developed, with EUIPO playing a central role in managing exclusion lists. Although non-binding, the resolution is expected to inform the Commission's review of the AI Act's Article 53 training-data summary obligations and could accelerate legislative proposals if voluntary compliance remains weak. For Prosus companies integrating generative AI — Brainly, GoStudent, Eruditus — understanding their AI suppliers' copyright compliance posture is now a governance priority.

ip.title: Getty Images v. Stability AI: Court Allows Trademark Claims Proceed While Dismissing DMCA False CMI Claim
ip.body: In early May 2026, coverage of Getty Images (US) Inc. v. Stability AI Ltd. confirmed that the court allowed Getty's trademark-related claims to proceed to the next stage, finding them sufficiently pled, while dismissing the Digital Millennium Copyright Act (DMCA) 'false copyright management information' claim without prejudice due to insufficient pleading of scienter — the intent element required under 17 U.S.C. § 1202. Stability AI had argued certain claims were improperly repackaged copyright infringement, but the court permitted the trademark channel to survive. The case continues to develop the legal framework for image-generative AI liability in the US and, alongside the Meta/publishers action, consolidates the litigation front against AI training and output practices. Prosus portfolio companies using image-generative AI tools must ensure their vendor contracts include IP indemnification clauses.

ip.title: EU AI Act: Commission Publishes Second Draft Code of Practice on Labelling AI-Generated Content — Rules Apply 2 August 2026
ip.body: The European Commission published a second draft of its Code of Practice on Marking and Labelling of AI-Generated Content in early 2026, with a stakeholder feedback deadline of 30 March 2026 and a final code expected by June 2026. The Commission confirmed that transparency rules for AI-generated content under the EU AI Act — covering deep-fake labelling and machine-readable disclosure of synthetic media — will become applicable on 2 August 2026. Separately, GPAI provider obligations to publish a sufficiently detailed summary of training data (Article 53 AI Act) became applicable on 2 August 2025. Prosus portfolio companies deploying AI-generated content in customer-facing services (Brainly, GoStudent, Eruditus, Advolve.AI) have a 2 August 2026 compliance deadline for output-labelling and must already be compliant with training-data summary requirements.
ip.source: European Commission / Shaping Europe's Digital Future

ai_landscape.title: US Commerce CAISI Expands Pre-Deployment AI Model Evaluations to Google DeepMind, Microsoft, and xAI
ai_landscape.body: On 5 May 2026, the US Department of Commerce's Center for AI Standards and Innovation (CAISI) expanded its pre-release model evaluation programme to include Google DeepMind, Microsoft, and xAI — joining OpenAI and Anthropic, which renegotiated their earlier evaluation agreements. Under these arrangements, AI companies provide CAISI with early access to frontier models — including versions with reduced safety guardrails — for assessment of capabilities in cybersecurity, biosecurity, and chemical weapons before public deployment. The expansion signals a de-facto US voluntary pre-deployment regime at scale, developed outside formal legislative action, and sets a precedent for analogous EU AI Office model evaluation procedures expected under Article 55 of the EU AI Act. Prosus portfolio companies evaluating or integrating frontier models from these providers should factor CAISI evaluation timelines into deployment planning.

ai_landscape.title: Anthropic Updates Responsible Scaling Policy (Version 3.2) Effective 29 April 2026
ai_landscape.body: On 29 April 2026, Anthropic published an updated Responsible Scaling Policy (RSP) Version 3.2, including revisions to its data retention principles — with Anthropic confirming it is undertaking a major internal project to improve data retention practices for future models and will publish its reasoning publicly. The RSP update also aligned the Noncompliance Reporting and Anti-Retaliation Policy with RSP Version 3.0 procedures. The update reflects Anthropic's commitment under the EU GPAI Code of Practice (Safety and Security Chapter, applicable from 2 August 2025) and its CAISI pre-deployment evaluation agreement. For Prosus entities that may integrate or build on Claude/Anthropic APIs — including Ema and Brainfish — the RSP's evolving safety requirements affect what capability commitments and deployment restrictions apply upstream.

ai_landscape.title: EU AI Act GPAI Code of Practice: Signatory Taskforce Meets Third Time in March 2026; Enforcement Window Opens August 2026
ai_landscape.body: The EU AI Office's Signatory Taskforce for the General-Purpose AI Code of Practice held its third meeting on 27 March 2026 (following sessions on 30 January and 13 March 2026), facilitating coherent application of the three-chapter Code among signatories including Amazon, Anthropic, Google, Microsoft, Mistral AI, and OpenAI. The Code's three chapters — Transparency (Article 53), Copyright (Article 53), and Safety/Security (Article 55 for systemic-risk models) — form the primary compliance vehicle for GPAI providers in the EU. The Commission confirmed that enforcement powers against GPAI providers under Chapter V of the AI Act will become active on 2 August 2026, creating a hard deadline for Prosus's AI-facing portfolio companies to confirm their AI supplier chains are covered by Code signatories or equivalent compliance documentation.

ai_landscape.title: White House Drafts Policy to Restore Federal Agency Access to Anthropic AI Systems
ai_landscape.body: On 29 April 2026, reporting confirmed that the White House was preparing draft guidance to permit federal agencies to resume use of Anthropic's AI systems, including the Mythos model, following months of tension between Anthropic and US defence officials over security designations and usage terms. The shift is reportedly being driven by urgency around frontier model cybersecurity evaluations — the same concern underlying CAISI's expanded pre-deployment assessment programme announced on 5 May 2026. The development reflects the US government's evolving balancing act between AI safety oversight and operational AI deployment at federal scale, with regulatory consequences for any AI providers seeking US government contracts. Prosus's AI portfolio companies — Ema, Brainfish, Advolve.AI — that compete in the enterprise and public-sector AI market should monitor how the federal access framework evolves.

ai_landscape.title: China Warns ByteDance Platforms for AI Content Labelling Violations Under Cybersecurity Law
ai_landscape.body: On 28 April 2026, China's Cyberspace Administration issued a formal warning to ByteDance platforms for violations of AI content labelling obligations under China's cybersecurity and algorithmic recommendation regulations, requiring platforms to apply machine-readable labels to AI-generated content. The warning is separate from ByteDance's ongoing US and EU regulatory challenges and signals that China is actively enforcing its domestic AI transparency rules — which predated the EU AI Act's analogous output-labelling obligations (applicable 2 August 2026). The ByteDance action sets a global enforcement precedent: multi-jurisdictional AI content labelling is now a live compliance requirement, not a forthcoming one. For Prosus portfolio companies using AI-generated content — Brainly, GoStudent, Advolve.AI — this reinforces the need for jurisdiction-aware labelling compliance frameworks.

ai_landscape.title: CMA Publishes Guidance on Consumer Law Compliance When Using AI Agents
ai_landscape.body: On 9 March 2026, the UK CMA published guidance titled 'Complying with Consumer Law When Using AI Agents', clarifying that businesses remain fully liable for consumer law violations committed by AI agents acting on their behalf — even where the agent is supplied by a third party. The guidance states businesses could face fines of up to 10% of worldwide turnover for breaches of consumer protection law enabled by AI agents. This is the first detailed UK regulatory guidance on agentic AI accountability and complements the CMA's broader digital-markets work. For Prosus portfolio companies deploying AI agents in customer-facing services — Ema (enterprise AI agent), Brainfish (customer support AI), Advolve.AI — the guidance creates direct liability exposure and compliance obligations under UK consumer law.
